text: The surgeon applies traction on the ileocolic pedicle to delineate the anatomical planes between the ileocolic pedicle, superior mesenteric artery, and vein. The dissection involves dividing the ileocolic artery near its origin from the superior mesenteric artery. Following the division, retroperitoneal structures are separated from the mesocolic fascia, with dissection extending medially to the C-shaped duodenum and the head of the pancreas, navigating multiple tissue planes.

The surgeon continues the medial to lateral dissection, revealing the fascia of Toldt. The dissection plane is maintained between the fascia of Toldt and the mesocolic fascia, protecting the right-sided ureter and gonadal vessels beneath the fascia layer. The right branch of the middle colic artery is dissected and divided at the appropriate level. The gastrocolic omentum is opened, staying close to the vascular arcade of the stomach.

The surgeon addresses adhesions in the greater omentum and gallbladder, entering the correct plane for dissection. The medial to lateral dissection in the infracolic compartment reaches the hepatic flexure, with the plane dissected from above after opening the gastrocolic omentum. The lateral side of the right colon is mobilized, connecting previous dissections from the supracolic and infracolic compartments. The final strands of the greater omentum at the hepatic flexure are divided, fully mobilizing the right colon. The greater omentum is divided along the proposed resection line of the transverse colon.

The surgeon divides the mesocolon up to the wall of the transverse colon, preparing for resection. The right branch of the middle colic artery is visible on the side of the specimen. After completing the division, the specimen is exteriorized, and an ileocolic anastomosis is performed.
